content.problemStatement:
“Animal instinct. When faced with danger, seek refuge in the herd.”

Jun-Ho has come to know about the escape passage route that VIPs use in case of emergency. Jun-Ho is in the leader's office and he is planning his escape from the building.

The building has a tree-like structure, with the leader's office as the root. Jun-Ho has obtained information on this structure, in the form of a tree on NNN nodes rooted at 111. The building has some security, which can be calculated as follows:


Each node of the tree has a value assigned to it — say, AiA_iAi​ for node iii.
Let uuu be a leaf node of the tree. The **security of uuu** is defined to be the bitwise OR of all values on the (unique) path from the root to uuu. Let this value be denoted LuL_uLu​.
The **security of the tree** is then defined to be the sum of the security of all its leaf nodes, i.e, the sum of LuL_uLu​ across all leaf nodes uuu of the tree.
The tree is considered to be secure when Lu>0L_u \gt 0Lu​>0 for every leaf node uuu of the tree; otherwise it is said to be insecure.

Jun-Ho would like the security of the tree to be as low as possible when making his escape. To this end, he can perform the following operation zero or more times:


Pick a node 1≤u≤N1 \leq u \leq N1≤u≤N of the tree, and set Au=0A_u = 0Au​=0.
However, if the tree ever becomes insecure as a result of an operation, the leader is immediately alerted and will foil Jun-Ho's plan. Thus, Jun-Ho **cannot perform an operation that would make the tree insecure**.

Help Jun-Ho find the minimum possible security the tree can have, such that the tree remains secure after every operation.

**Note**:


A leaf is a node of the tree without any child nodes.
For N=1N = 1N=1, the root is also considered to be a leaf.
Given input tree will be secured tree.

Input Format

The first line of input contains a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN, denoting the number of nodes of the tree.
The next N−1N-1N−1 lines each contain two space-separated integers uuu and vvv, denoting an edge between nodes uuu and vvv in the tree.
The next line contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​ — the values of the nodes.


Output Format
For each test case, output a single integer denoting the minimum security of the tree.
constraints: The graph given in the input will always be a secured tree
Sum of NNN over all test cases does not exceed 10610^6106.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test Case 1: No operation can be done, and the minimum security of the tree is thus 555.
Test Case 2: Set A1A_1A1​ and A3A_3A3​ to 000. It can be seen that L2=2L_2 = 2L2​=2, L4=1L_4 = 1L4​=1 and L5=4L_5 = 4L5​=4; so every leaf  has positive security. The security of the tree is 2+1+4=72 + 1 + 4 = 72+1+4=7, and this is the minimum attainable.

Test Case 3: The given tree already has minimum security, that being 666.
